nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rivers with rocky substrates near the source and muddy/sandy substrates with dense aquatic vegetation in slower reaches; overhanging shore vegetation; clear, well-oxygenated water in the Río Coy system of the upper Río Pánuco basin, San Luis Potosí

decorPreferences: dense planting along sides and background (Java moss, hornwort, water sprite)
smooth rocks and pebbles replicating fast-water biotope
overhanging or floating plants for surface cover
gentle to moderate current from filtration

diet.notes: Wild fish graze on aufwuchs, microalgae, and zooplankton. In the aquarium, offer a varied diet of high-quality micro-pellets or flake supplemented with small live and frozen foods — daphnia, artemia/brine shrimp, cyclops, and mosquito larvae. Occasional spirulina-based or algae flake helps replicate the aufwuchs component. Juveniles benefit from freshly hatched artemia nauplii and finely powdered dry food.

goodWith: other small peaceful livebearers of similar size
small rasboras and tetras that prefer harder water
avoidWith: larger, boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding
large shrimp species likely to predate fry (neocaridina acceptable with dense planting)

summary: The Pygmy Swordtail is a small, cool-tolerant livebearer from the Río Pánuco basin of Mexico. Males carry a short sword extension and come in two genetically fixed size classes — large 'courter' males and small 'sneaker' males — that pursue different mating tactics, making the species a well-known model in evolutionary biology.
careNotes:
The Pygmy Swordtail (Xiphophorus multilineatus) is a small livebearer endemic to the Río Coy system of the upper Río Pánuco drainage in San Luis Potosí, Mexico, where it lives in fast-flowing, spring-fed water that stays relatively cool and clean year round. That cool, hard-water origin sets it apart from most tropical livebearers: the species tolerates temperatures as low as 18°C and does well between 22 and 25°C, making it a good fit for unheated or lightly heated tanks in temperate rooms. Hard, alkaline water in the range of pH 7.0 to 8.0 and gH 10 to 25 is essential, and soft or acidic conditions tend to be poorly tolerated, leading to increased disease susceptibility.

Despite the modest 60 L minimum tank volume, a species-only or near-species-only setup is strongly recommended. The Pygmy Swordtail is unusually stress-sensitive and inclined to hide among plants, and it may fail to feed if outcompeted by faster or more assertive tankmates. Strong filtration that produces a gentle current, small and frequent water changes of around 20 to 25 percent weekly, and stable parameters matter more for this species than for most hobby livebearers.

The Pygmy Swordtail is best known for a striking male size polymorphism — that is, two distinct adult body forms determined genetically rather than by environment — with the underlying genes located on the Y chromosome. The larger 'courter' males (25 to 42 mm) are deeper-bodied, develop the characteristic high back and a bold zigzag stripe along the flank, and rely on persistent courtship displays to attract females. The smaller 'sneaker' males (22 to 28 mm) are more streamlined and use a mix of courtship and opportunistic sneak-chase copulation attempts. Females generally prefer courter males, but the strength of that preference varies with the female's own genotype and growth rate, a system that has generated decades of research into sexual selection and female mate choice. In the aquarium, keeping a surplus of females reduces harassment, and a ratio of two to three females per male is advisable. A small group of one to two males with three to five females tends to do well in 60 to 80 L of densely planted water.

The trade name 'Pygmy Swordtail' is also applied to the related X. pygmaeus, the Slender Pygmy Swordtail; X. multilineatus is distinguished by the high back in large males, prominent vertical barring on the flanks, and a slightly stockier build. See also: [swordtail] (Xiphophorus helleri), the common large Swordtail of the hobby; [platy] (Xiphophorus maculatus), a close relative in the family Poeciliidae.
breedingNotes: The Pygmy Swordtail is a livebearer, meaning females retain the eggs internally and release fully formed young rather than spawning. Gestation lasts approximately 24 to 28 days and produces small broods of 5 to 20 fry, typically around 10 to 15. Females are also able to store sperm from a single mating and produce several successive broods without further contact with a male. The fry are relatively large at birth and tend to be ignored by adults in a well-planted tank, though moving gravid females or newborns to a separate fry container improves survival rates. Juveniles generally reach sexual maturity at around four months, and courter males develop their full adult coloration within the first year. Because male size class — courter or sneaker — is fixed genetically and expressed during growth rather than at maturity, keepers interested in seeing both forms should retain fish from several broods rather than relying on conditioning to produce one type. Breeding is rated moderate overall: the species breeds readily once well settled, but its stress sensitivity and preference for a quiet, species-only setup mean it rarely produces the large numbers seen with hardier livebearers such as guppies or platies.

primary.name: FishBase — Xiphophorus multilineatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed max TL males 4.5 cm, females 5.0 cm; temperature 23–25°C; native range Rio Coy of Rio Panuco basin, San Luis Potosi, Mexico; habitat fast-flowing rivers with dense aquatic vegetation; livebearer gestation 24–28 days, 10–15 young; taxonomy Poeciliidae, Cyprinodontiformes; described Rauchenberger, Kallman & Morizot 1990.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Xiphophorus multilineatus
additional.notes: Confirmed minimum tank 60 cm × 40 cm × 30 cm; temperature 23–25°C; diet live/frozen artemia, cyclops, daphnia, mosquito larvae and high-protein dry food; stress sensitivity requiring species-only setup; males 25–40 mm, females 40 mm; broods 10–20 fry monthly; juveniles mature in 4 months, full color in 1 year.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Pygmy Swordtail, Xiphophorus multilineatus
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 22–26°C (71.5–78°F); pH 7.0–8.0; hard alkaline water requirement; diet aufwuchs, algae, zooplankton in wild; accepts daphnia, brine shrimp, flake in aquarium; rocky boulder-filled tank with good water movement; recommended more females than males; broods 10–20 fry.

additional.name: British Livebearer Association — Xiphophorus multilineatus
additional.notes: Confirmed endemic range: Rio Coy system including Arroyo Tambaque and Arroyo Oxitipa, Rio Panuco drainage, SLP Mexico; four distinct male size variants; habitat fast-flowing river with dense plant growth and overhanging vegetation; fry large and normally left alone by parents; requires large well-planted tank with good filtration and aeration.

additional.name: PLOS ONE — Genotype and growth rate influence female mate preference in Xiphophorus multilineatus
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed source for male polymorphism biology. Confirmed courter males (25–42 mm, deeper-bodied, fixed courtship) vs. sneaker males (22–28 mm, fusiform, use both courtship and sneak-chase copulation); male size class determined by Mc4r gene alleles on Y chromosome; female preference strength influenced by female genotype and growth rate.

fish.breedingNotes: The Pygmy Swordtail is a livebearer with a gestation of around 24 to 28 days and broods of 5 to 20 fry, typically around 10 to 15. Females can store sperm and produce several successive broods from a single mating. The fry are large at birth and tend to be left alone in well-planted tanks. Juveniles generally reach maturity at about four months, and courter males develop full coloration within the first year. Both male forms — courter and sneaker — should be expected in a breeding group, since male size class is fixed genetically rather than triggered by environmental conditions.
